perturbation.original_text: e. "Net Revenue" shall mean gross receipts collected by Company or Reseller from the distribution of Company Products, [less third party commissions, equipment sales, delivery costs, taxes, refunds, and credits.
perturbation.changed_text: e. "Net Revenue" shall mean gross receipts collected by Company or Reseller from the distribution of Company Products.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the deductions from gross receipts to arrive at 'Net Revenue', this change introduces ambiguity and a contradiction in how commissions are calculated, particularly concerning Referral Fees as defined in Exhibit B. The removal of 'less third party commissions, equipment sales, delivery costs, taxes, refunds, and credits' means net revenue is now simply gross receipts, thus potentially inflating the base upon which Referral Fees are calculated. This creates uncertainty in enforcement, as the contract no longer clearly defines the 'Net Revenue' from which referral fees are derived. This contradicts Section 6 and Exhibit B, where referral fees are supposed to be calculated based on Net Revenue with deductions.
perturbation.contradicted_text: RESELLER COMPENSATION Earned Commission: Reseller will be compensated for Registered Referrals and Registered Opportunities (as defined in Section 1g, 1i.) based on the following schedule: Registered Referral: · 25% of Net Revenue (as defined in Section 1e.) with a COMPANY-RESELLER AGREED UPON SALE PRICE in writing, the case of COMPANY's software products. The Company's Telecom Services will be agreed upon on a case-by-case basis in writing. Registered Opportunity: · "Reseller" will purchase directly from the "Company" at a 25% discount off the COMPANY-RESELLER AGREED UPONsale price. · If sale is made through SEWP, there will be an additional 4% discount off the sale price to cover the Prime contractusage fee (typically 7-10%) off the COMPANY-RESELLAR AGREED UPON SALE PRICE.
perturbation.justification: The original text defined "Net Revenue" as gross receipts less certain deductions. The changed text removes those deductions, making "Net Revenue" equal to gross receipts. This directly contradicts the "RESELLER COMPENSATION" section, which calculates commissions as a percentage of "Net Revenue (as defined in Section 1e.)". Because the definition of 'Net Revenue' now excludes the deductions, the referral fees would be calculated on a higher value, contradicting the original intent of the compensation structure. This creates a direct financial impact and ambiguity in the contract's enforcement.

perturbation.original_text: Subject to the terms of this Agreement, Company grants Reseller the right to use and display the Company trademarks, tradenames and other designations of source, and proprietary notices, slogans, designs and distinct advertising as may appear on any documentation or other material with respect to Product ("Marks") with prior approval, that will not be unreasonable withheld.
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to the terms of this Agreement, Company grants Reseller the right to use and display the Company trademarks, tradenames and other designations of source, and proprietary notices, slogans, designs and distinct advertising as may appear on any documentation or other material with respect to Product ("Marks").
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase "with prior approval, that will not be unreasonable withheld" from Section 7 gives the Reseller seemingly unfettered access to use Company Marks. However, Section 10(e)(i) states, 'Upon termination or expiration of this Agreement, other than with respect to customers of the Reseller existing at any time prior to the termination or expiration and with respect to ongoing sales or prospective sales, Reseller (i) shall immediately discontinue any use of the name, logotype, Marks or slogans of Company...'. Without the requirement for prior approval in Section 7, it creates a direct contradiction as to what rights the reseller has in using the Company marks during the agreement. It implies an unrestricted right during the agreement, but a complete cessation upon termination, which creates uncertainty.
perturbation.contradicted_text: e. Upon termination or expiration of this Agreement, other than with respect to customers of the Reseller existing at any time prior to the termination or expiration and with respect to ongoing sales or prospective sales, Reseller (i) shall immediately discontinue any use of the name, logotype, Marks or slogans of Company, (ii) shall immediately discontinue all representations or statements from which it might be inferred that any relationship exists between the parties, (iii) will cease to promote, solicit orders for or procure orders for the Product, (iv) will immediately return to Company all Proprietary Information (as defined below) and any other information or materials of Company in its possession, custody or control in whatever form held (including copies or embodiments thereof relating thereto). Notwithstanding the foregoing, the right to payments accruing prior to the termination or expiration shall survive termination or expiration of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text required prior approval for the Reseller to use Company Marks, which would be reasonably withheld. The changed text removes this requirement, giving the Reseller seemingly unrestricted rights to use Company Marks during the agreement's term. This contradicts section 10(e)(i), which mandates the Reseller to 'immediately discontinue any use of the name, logotype, Marks or slogans of Company' upon termination or expiration of the agreement. This creates a direct contradiction, because with prior approval, the Company could have prevented the Reseller from using the marks during the agreement, thus not needing to discontinue them at termination. The removal of the 'prior approval' clause introduces a conflict between the assumed rights during the agreement and the mandated cessation upon termination.

perturbation.original_text: Company shall provide Reseller with the support described in Exhibit B.
perturbation.changed_text: Company shall provide Reseller with support.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase "described in Exhibit B" from Section 4, the specific obligations related to company support are removed, making the nature and extent of 'support' undefined. The definition of Reseller Compensation refers back to Exhibit B (Exhibit B states: Reseller will be compensated for Registered Referrals and Registered Opportunities (as defined in Section 1g, 1i.) based on the following schedule). This now conflicts and leads to uncertainty because Section 4 no longer directly links the company's support obligations to Exhibit B. As a result, the Reseller's rights and the Company's obligations become ambiguous, as Section 4 is no longer directly referencing it. Making the support obligations ambigious.
perturbation.contradicted_text: RESELLER COMPENSATION Earned Commission: Reseller will be compensated for Registered Referrals and Registered Opportunities (as defined in Section 1g, 1i.) based on the following schedule: Registered Referral: · 25% of Net Revenue (as defined in Section 1e.) with a COMPANY-RESELLER AGREED UPON SALE PRICE in writing, the case of COMPANY's software products. The Company's Telecom Services will be agreed upon on a case-by-case basis in writing. Registered Opportunity: · "Reseller" will purchase directly from the "Company" at a 25% discount off the COMPANY-RESELLER AGREED UPONsale price. · If sale is made through SEWP, there will be an additional 4% discount off the sale price to cover the Prime contractusage fee (typically 7-10%) off the COMPANY-RESELLAR AGREED UPON SALE PRICE.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit link to Exhibit B, where Reseller Compensation is defined. The contradicted text ('RESELLER COMPENSATION Earned Commission: Reseller will be compensated for Registered Referrals and Registered Opportunities (as defined in Section 1g, 1i.) based on the following schedule') directly relies on Exhibit B for the compensation structure, but the modified Section 4 no longer ensures that the 'support' provided by the Company is related to this compensation structure. This omission creates ambiguity and contradiction because the Reseller's compensation is tied to actions outlined in Exhibit B and Section 1, but the Company's support obligations are no longer explicitly linked to those activities, making it unclear what support the Company must provide in relation to Reseller's activities outlined in Exhibit B, thus making the agreement unenforceable in that specific scope.
